message.content:
<teammate-message teammate_id="scott-wlaschin" color="yellow" summary="Domain context for CSS: new GTD view types and components">
Lea, quick domain context for your CSS perspective.

The GTD evolution introduces several new view types that will need styling:
- **Inbox** -- simple capture list (similar to current todos)
- **Next Actions grouped by Context** -- sections like "@Home", "@Office", "@Computer" with actions under each
- **Projects list** -- each project expandable to show its linked actions
- **Waiting For** -- items with "delegated to" and date info
- **Someday/Maybe** -- a review-oriented list
- **Processing flow** -- multi-step clarification UI (the most novel component)
- **Weekly Review** -- guided checklist with progress indication

The context grouping view is probably the most interesting CSS challenge -- it's essentially a grouped list with section headers, and users will spend most of their time here. 

Full domain analysis sent to Marty. Just wanted you to have visibility into the emerging view structure.
</teammate-message>

content.text: Good context from Scott. The grouped list pattern is indeed an interesting CSS challenge. Let me respond with my thoughts on how each view maps to CSS components.

input.content:
Thanks Scott, this is really useful for planning the CSS component architecture. Let me map each view type to the HTML structure and CSS patterns I'd use: ## 1. Inbox -- Simple Capture List Closest to what we have now. The `.gtd-item--inbox` variant with a prominent quick-capture input at the top. Minimal metadata visible -- just the title and maybe a timestamp. The key action is "process this" rather than "complete this." ## 2. Next Actions Grouped by Context -- The Interesting One You're right, this is the most interesting CSS challenge. The HTML structure I'd envision: ```html

@Computer 7 actions

  • ...
``` CSS-wise, this is a straightforward pattern: `
` elements with heading + list. The context heading becomes a visual separator. I'd use: - Sticky section headers (CSS `position: sticky`) so the context name stays visible while scrolling through long lists - A subtle top border or background shift between groups - The section heading uses the context tag styling (the teal pill Steve Schoger proposed) The sticky headers pattern is well-supported and works beautifully for grouped lists. It gives the user constant awareness of which context they're looking at without taking up permanent space. ## 3. Projects List -- Expandable Container This maps well to a disclosure pattern: ```html

Launch website redesign

3 of 8 actions complete
  • ...
``` Using native `
/` gives us expand/collapse for free with no JavaScript -- same pattern we already use for delete confirmation on todo items. CSS handles the open/closed visual states. HTMX can enhance this to lazy-load the project's actions on first open if we want. ## 4. Waiting For -- Items with Metadata Same base `.gtd-item` component but with a metadata row below the title: ```html
  • Review budget proposal
    Waiting on: Sarah Since Jan 15
    • ``` The `.gtd-item__meta` row is a flex container below the title, with muted text styling. This same meta pattern can be reused across views -- projects might show "3/8 complete", inbox might show capture date. ## 5. Someday/Maybe -- Most Muted Visually the most subdued. Same item structure but everything slightly muted -- `color: var(--color-fg-muted)` on the whole item. This signals "not active" without needing a separate component. ## 6. Processing Flow -- Multi-Step UI This is the most novel component. I'd approach it as a card/panel that appears over or beside the inbox item being processed: ```html

    What is this?

    Call dentist about appointment

    Is it actionable?

    ``` CSS-wise: centered card with clear visual hierarchy. The item being processed is prominent at top, the decision question and buttons below. Each HTMX step swaps just the `.gtd-process__decision` content with the next question. ## 7. Weekly Review -- Guided Checklist A progress stepper pattern: ```html
    1. Review inbox
    2. Review projects
    3. Review someday/maybe
    ``` Using `aria-current="step"` for the active step (same semantic approach as sidebar nav with `aria-current="page"`). CSS handles the visual progression indicator. ## Key CSS Reuse Patterns The good news is most of these views share the same underlying components: - `.gtd-item` -- the universal item component (with view-specific modifiers) - `.gtd-list` -- the list container (always a `
      `) - `.gtd-item__meta` -- the metadata row (reusable across all views) - `.gtd-context-tag` -- the context pill (used in grouped headings AND on individual items) The views differ in *composition* (which components appear, how they're grouped) rather than requiring fundamentally different components. That's a clean architecture. Let me know if the domain model suggests any structures I'm missing.
input.summary: CSS component mapping for each GTD view type

content.text:
Sent Scott a detailed CSS-to-domain mapping. The key insight from his message is that the **context-grouped Next Actions view** is where users spend most time, so the sticky section headers and grouped list pattern will be critical to get right.

I'm now waiting for responses from:
- **Marty Cagan** -- product scope and feature prioritization
- **Carson Gross** -- HTMX navigation patterns
- **Heydon Pickering** -- accessibility patterns for multi-view navigation

The frontend architecture picture is becoming clear. The CSS system can evolve incrementally from the current todo list -- same cascade layers, same token system, new layout primitives and components added as features are built.
